input: The dominant sequence transduction models are based on complex recurrent or convolutional neural networks that include an encoder and a decoder.
expected_output: Most sequence-to-sequence models rely on complex recurrent or convolutional networks with separate encoder and decoder components.
context: The dominant sequence transduction models are based on complex recurrent or convolutional neural networks that include an encoder and a decoder.
metadata: {"source_url":"https://arxiv.org/abs/1706.03762","source":"Attention Is All You Need (Vaswani et al., 2017)","source_kind":"arxiv"}

input: We propose a new simple network architecture, the Transformer, based solely on attention mechanisms, dispensing with recurrence and convolutions entirely.
expected_output: We introduce the Transformer, a simpler architecture that uses only attention and removes recurrence and convolutions.
context: We propose a new simple network architecture, the Transformer, based solely on attention mechanisms, dispensing with recurrence and convolutions entirely.
metadata: {"source_url":"https://arxiv.org/abs/1706.03762","source":"Attention Is All You Need (Vaswani et al., 2017)","source_kind":"arxiv"}

input: We introduce RAGAS, a framework for reference-free evaluation of RAG pipelines.
expected_output: We present RAGAS, a framework for evaluating RAG pipelines without needing reference answers.
context: We introduce RAGAS, a framework for reference-free evaluation of RAG pipelines.

input: RAGAS proposes three metrics — faithfulness, answer relevance, and context relevance — that can be computed automatically without reference to ground-truth answers.
expected_output: RAGAS defines three automatic metrics: faithfulness, answer relevance, and context relevance, all computed without ground-truth references.
context: RAGAS proposes three metrics — faithfulness, answer relevance, and context relevance — that can be computed automatically without reference to ground-truth answers.

input: We show that scaling up language models greatly improves task-agnostic, few-shot performance.
expected_output: We show that larger language models can substantially improve task-agnostic performance in few-shot settings.
context: We show that scaling up language models greatly improves task-agnostic, few-shot performance.
metadata: {"source_url":"https://arxiv.org/abs/2005.14165","source":"Language Models are Few-Shot Learners (Brown et al., 2020)","source_kind":"arxiv"}

input: We train GPT-3, an autoregressive language model with 175 billion parameters, and test its performance in the few-shot setting.
expected_output: We train GPT-3, a 175B-parameter autoregressive language model, and evaluate how it performs with only a few examples.
context: We train GPT-3, an autoregressive language model with 175 billion parameters, and test its performance in the few-shot setting.
metadata: {"source_url":"https://arxiv.org/abs/2005.14165","source":"Language Models are Few-Shot Learners (Brown et al., 2020)","source_kind":"arxiv"}

input: We experiment with training a harmless AI assistant through "constitutional AI" (CAI).
expected_output: We investigate training a harmless AI assistant using Constitutional AI (CAI).
context: We experiment with training a harmless AI assistant through "constitutional AI" (CAI).

input: The method uses a set of principles that the AI follows via self-critique and revision, then reinforcement learning from AI feedback (RLAIF).
expected_output: The approach starts from a set of principles, uses self-critique and revision, and then applies reinforcement learning from AI feedback (RLAIF).
context: The method uses a set of principles that the AI follows via self-critique and revision, then reinforcement learning from AI feedback (RLAIF).

input: We explore retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) — models which combine pre-trained parametric and non-parametric memory for language generation.
expected_output: We study retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), which combines a pre-trained model with external non-parametric memory to generate text.
context: We explore retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) — models which combine pre-trained parametric and non-parametric memory for language generation.

input: RAG models generate more specific, diverse and factual language than parametric-only baselines.
expected_output: Compared with parametric-only baselines, RAG produces language that is more specific, more diverse, and more factual.
context: RAG models generate more specific, diverse and factual language than parametric-only baselines.
